{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 60-year-old chronic smoker with progressive jaundice, pruritus, and clay-colored stools for 2 months. The history of waxing and waning jaundice is also present. A CT scan revealed dilated main pancreatic duct and common bile duct. These clinical features suggest an obstruction in the biliary or pancreatic ducts. The differential diagnoses include ampullary carcinoma, bile duct tumors, gallbladder cancer, pancreatic cancer, and cholangiocarcinoma. Given the dilated pancreatic and common bile ducts, the obstruction is likely at the ampulla of Vater. The most common cause of such an obstruction is a periampullary carcinoma, which includes carcinomas of the head of the pancreas and the ampulla of Vater. Chronic pancreatitis can also cause ductal dilation but is less likely given the progressive nature of the symptoms and the dilated common bile duct. Bile duct strictures and cholangiocarcinoma are also possible but less likely given the clinical presentation. Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is periampullary carcinoma.", "answer_choice": "B"}
